attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of malodorous feet and mildly tender, pruritic heels. She works as a hiking tour guide five days of the week. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple widespread small, brown and white shallow depressions on the pressure-bearing areas of both heels which are slightly tender. There is an associated unpleasant smell but no erythema, scaling, or vesicles.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of pitted keratolysis, a superficial bacterial infection of the stratum corneum caused by Corynebacterium species. The characteristic findings include multiple small pits on weight-bearing areas of the soles, malodour, and association with occlusive footwear as a hiker. The pits are typically white but can also be brown, and it can be tender and itchy. It can also occur on the palms of the hands in certain occupations. Lifestyle modifications are critical to management and prevention.

Topical erythromycin is the most appropriate treatment. The antibiotic addresses the bacterial infection. Treatment should also include lifestyle modifications such as wearing moisture-wicking socks, using antiperspirant foot powder or deoderant, changing socks regularly, and allowing shoes to dry between use to help prevent recurrence by reducing moisture and bacterial growth.

Topical terbinafine would be ineffective as this is an antifungal medication, and pitted keratolysis is a bacterial infection. Additionally, addressing predisposing factors through lifestyle modification is essential for successful treatment.

Cryotherapy would be inappropriate and potentially harmful for this condition. Whilst the depressions could be thought to be due to plantar warts, the presence of odour and the characteristics of the depressions make this less likely. Cryotherapy would not address the underlying bacterial infection and could damage healthy tissue without treating the cause.

Oral fluconazole is inappropriate as it is an antifungal medication and would not address the bacterial cause. Furthermore, systemic therapy is not necessary for this superficial infection.

Topical hydrocortisone would be inappropriate as corticosteroids do not address the underlying bacterial infection and may potentially worsen the condition by suppressing local immune responses.
learningPoints: Treatment involves both topical antibiotics and addressing predisposing factors such as excessive sweating and prolonged occlusive footwear.
Maintaining dry feet and using breathable footwear are essential preventive measures.
references: Lam C, Mitchell G. Pitted keratolysis. DermNet Nz. 2022. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/pitted-keratolysis
Morais P. Smelly foot rash. Australian Family Physician. 2011: 40 (3). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/4e7115d8-019b-4719-98b1-6f8ebf484a4e/Smelly-foot-rash.aspx
Saravanan R, Baalan K. Pitted Keratloysis. Pan Africal Medical Journal. 2022. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9250681/
